requirement_text: A governance framework MUST identify the classes of governed entities within its scope.
Where a governed entity is a machine, software component or agent, the framework MUST identify one or more accountable persons or organisations and the basis of their accountability.
A framework MUST define each governed actor role, including its responsibilities, eligibility, authority limits, obligations and lifecycle.
A framework permitting agent participation MUST state which agent classes are permitted and which effects each class may request, recommend, approve or perform.
A role definition MUST specify the conditions under which the role is assigned, activated, suspended, transferred and terminated.
Every authority recognised by a framework MUST identify its source.
Authority MUST be bounded by sufficient scope information to determine whether a proposed effect falls within it.

Identification, authentication, possession of a credential, registry inclusion or technical capability MUST NOT be treated as sufficient proof of authority unless the framework explicitly defines and validates the authority semantics represented by that artifact or state.
A framework MUST distinguish capability from authority.
Systems enforcing a framework MUST NOT admit a consequential effect solely because an actor possesses the technical means to cause it.
A delegation MUST identify the delegator, delegate, delegated authority, effective period and termination conditions.
Redelegation MUST be expressly authorised.
Each delegation hop MUST NOT enlarge the authority available at the parent hop unless an independently valid authority source authorises the enlargement.
Delegated authority MUST remain traceable to its authority source and, where applicable, to the originating principal.
Suspension, revocation or expiry of parent authority MUST affect descendant authority according to explicit propagation rules.
Delegation MUST NOT extinguish the accountability of a delegator, principal, operator, provider or other party whose accountability is established by law, agreement or framework policy.
A framework MUST define how conflicts among permissions, prohibitions, obligations and constraints are resolved.
A framework MUST identify the authority under which a permission, prohibition, obligation or constraint is established.
Machine-actionable rules MUST be traceable to their normative policy source and the authority that approved that source.
A governance framework MUST define precedence when human-readable normative text and machine-actionable representations conflict.
Unless the framework explicitly designates a machine-actionable representation as co-normative and defines equivalence tests, the human-readable normative text MUST prevail.
A framework MUST define the classes of claims that may be relied upon and the evidence, provenance, status and assurance needed for each class.
Evidence relied upon for a consequential decision MUST have sufficient provenance to identify its origin and relevant transformations.
A framework MUST define freshness requirements for evidence whose relevance may change over time.
An inability to validate required evidence MUST result in denial, restriction, suspension or human review according to framework policy; it MUST NOT silently result in acceptance.
A framework MUST distinguish verification results from broader assurance conclusions.
Verification and assessment results MUST identify the criteria, evaluator, time, scope, limitations and evidence used.
Assurance MUST NOT be represented as permanent where the underlying conditions, evidence or system state may change.
A framework MUST define the validity, renewal, suspension, withdrawal and review conditions of each assurance conclusion it recognises.
A trust decision MUST be attributable to a decision-maker or accountable decision service.
A trust path, credential, registry entry, reputation value or assurance mark MUST NOT by itself constitute a trust decision.
A decision MUST be evaluated in the context of the relying party’s applicable policy, purpose and risk tolerance.
A framework MUST identify the consequential effect classes within its scope.
A consequential effect MUST NOT be admitted unless the applicable authority, policy, evidence and runtime conditions have been evaluated to the level required by the framework.
A framework MUST identify the governance contexts in which its requirements apply.
Cross-context reliance MUST be governed by explicit recognition, mapping or conflict-resolution rules.
A framework MUST define the governance events that materially affect authority, assurance, status, eligibility, policy or accountability.
Material governance events MUST be attributable, time-stamped and reconstructable.
Every consequential effect MUST have at least one identifiable accountable party.
Machine actors and agents MUST NOT be treated as the terminal point of accountability.
A framework MUST define how accountability is allocated when multiple actors, agents, services, registries or authorities contribute to an effect.
A framework MUST identify material harms that may arise from governed effects, including cumulative and systemic harms where applicable.
A framework governing consequential effects MUST define accessible challenge, review and remedy mechanisms.
A relationship used in an automated trust decision MUST have an unambiguous type and declared semantics.
A relationship MUST NOT imply broader authority or recognition than its declared scope.
Recognition MUST NOT be treated as delegation unless the relationship explicitly grants authority.
Accreditation MUST NOT be treated as a guarantee of every output produced by the accredited actor.
Registry inclusion MUST NOT be treated as universal trustworthiness.
Revocation and suspension semantics MUST identify the affected relationship, effective time, authority and propagation consequences.
A conforming governance framework MUST include a Primary Document and an authoritative Schedule of Controlled Documents.
The Primary Document MUST identify the exact framework version using a persistent identifier.
The framework MUST publish a conformance statement identifying the profiles and conformance targets claimed.
The framework MUST identify its official language or languages and SHOULD use BCP 47 language tags.
Where multiple official versions exist, the framework MUST define how inconsistencies are resolved.
The framework MUST identify where decision rights, enforcement powers, revision powers and emergency powers reside.
Where no single governing authority exists, the framework MUST identify the authority topology and the process by which valid governance decisions are formed.
Every framework MUST declare whether agents may participate and, if so, whether they may recommend, decide, transact, delegate, use tools, create sub-agents or produce binding effects.
Governance state required for a runtime decision MUST be discoverable, authenticated and sufficiently current for the decision context.
The framework MUST define how revisions are proposed, reviewed, approved, versioned, published, deprecated and withdrawn.
A publicly available framework SHOULD include a public review period for substantive revisions.
Material policy changes affecting active authority or runtime decisions MUST define transition and notification rules.
A framework MUST identify conflicts that may arise among authorities and the procedure for resolving or containing them.
Where authorities overlap, the framework MUST define precedence, coordination, refusal or escalation semantics.
A delegation MUST NOT become active before required acceptance, registration, verification or other activation conditions are met.
The framework MUST define whether delegation is transferable and whether an agent or actor may substitute another delegate.
Material substitution of an agent, model, operator, toolchain or execution environment MUST trigger reassessment where it may affect authority, assurance or risk.
A multi-hop delegation decision MUST validate each active hop, parent-child scope compatibility, originating-principal continuity, redelegation permission and revocation state.
A framework MUST define maximum delegation depth or the policy by which depth is determined.
Where authority is divided among multiple delegates, the framework MUST define whether branches are independent, cumulative, mutually exclusive or jointly controlled.
Where delegated branches converge on one effect, the aggregate effect MUST be evaluated against the originating authority and applicable aggregate limits.
Emergency authority MUST be narrowly scoped, time-limited, attributable, reviewable and subject to post-event accountability.
A framework governing consequential effects MUST define the minimum runtime governance envelope required for each effect class.
A runtime decision MUST fail safely when required authority, policy, evidence, status or context cannot be validated.
Runtime governance MUST evaluate current revocation and suspension state to the freshness required by the effect’s risk.
Outcome semantics MUST be unambiguous to the enforcement point.
Each consequential effect MUST produce or reference a decision receipt proportionate to the effect’s risk and accountability requirements.
A receipt MUST NOT expose confidential evidence beyond what is necessary for authorised audit, review or challenge.
The framework MUST define retention, access, disclosure, correction and deletion rules for receipts.
Where authority, risk or evidence may materially change during execution, the framework MUST define conditions for re-evaluation, interruption or containment.
Runtime revocation MUST be capable of stopping or constraining ongoing activity where technically and legally feasible.
A framework MUST define the attributes needed to identify an agent for governance purposes.
A framework MUST define when changes to model, instructions, memory, tools, operator, provider, execution environment or policy configuration constitute a material change of agent state or identity.
Material changes MUST trigger re-registration, renewed assurance, restricted operation or another declared lifecycle response where necessary.
An agent performing an effect on behalf of a principal MUST carry or reference verifiable authority for that effect.
The framework MUST identify the principal, operator, provider, deployer and supervisor roles applicable to each agent class and allocate accountability among them.
Agent authority SHOULD be bound to a task, purpose or effect class wherever practical.
Tool access SHOULD be limited to what is necessary for the authorised task.
An agent MUST NOT infer authority from mere access to accounts, credentials, data, APIs or tools.
An agent or supervising service MUST evaluate applicable governance constraints before executing a consequential plan step.
Where an agent cannot determine whether a planned effect is authorised, it MUST deny, restrict or escalate rather than assume permission.
An agent MUST NOT create a sub-agent, substitute another agent or redelegate authority unless explicitly permitted.
A sub-agent MUST receive no more authority than the delegating agent is authorised to redelegate.
The originating principal and accountable-party chain MUST remain discoverable across sub-agent relationships.
A framework permitting multi-agent coordination MUST define how authority, task state, evidence, obligations and accountability are partitioned and reconciled.
Coordination protocols MUST NOT be assumed to provide governance semantics unless those semantics are explicitly defined and validated.
Where multiple agents contribute to one effect, the decision receipt MUST identify the material contributions and accountable parties.
Tools capable of producing consequential effects MUST enforce or receive sufficient authority and policy context to prevent unauthorised invocation.
Tool outputs used as evidence MUST carry provenance and integrity appropriate to the decision.
A framework requiring human oversight MUST specify who performs it, when it occurs, what information is available, and what intervention powers exist.
A guardian or supervisory agent MUST itself be governed by explicit authority, constraints and accountability.
A framework MUST define mechanisms to suspend, isolate, contain or terminate an agent when authority, assurance or safety conditions fail.
Termination MUST address outstanding tasks, delegated authority, retained data, tools, obligations and evidence preservation.
Graph relationships used for automated decisions MUST be typed, scoped, attributable, time-bounded where applicable and linked to status or revocation semantics.
A graph path MUST be treated as evidence for a decision, not as the decision itself.
Each material graph assertion MUST identify the asserting party and authority basis.
A graph implementation MUST provide integrity and provenance sufficient to detect unauthorised alteration of relied-upon assertions.
Derived edges or computed trust values MUST identify their derivation method, input sources and applicable policy.
A framework MUST define permitted traversal depth, relationship types, path constraints and evidence thresholds for each automated decision class.
A relying party MUST apply its own policy to determine whether a discovered path is sufficient.
A framework MUST define how conflicting, stale, superseded or contested graph assertions are represented and evaluated.
Absence of a negative assertion MUST NOT automatically be interpreted as positive trust unless the framework explicitly justifies that inference.
Registry inclusion MUST have explicit semantics and MUST NOT be represented as general trustworthiness.
A registry framework MUST define who may create, update, suspend, revoke, correct and archive each record class.
Registry records used for decisions MUST expose sufficient status, provenance, authority and version information.
A registry MUST provide a correction and dispute path for materially incorrect or unauthorised records.
Federation MUST distinguish data replication, technical interoperability, recognition of authority and reliance upon determinations.
Recognition of another registry MUST state the accepted record classes, purposes, assurance thresholds, jurisdictional limits and withdrawal conditions.
The framework MUST define what each assurance form establishes and what it does not establish.
Assurance providers MUST disclose material conflicts of interest and the basis of their competence and authority.
Assurance results MUST be challengeable and correctable when materially wrong.
A Continuous Assurance Profile framework MUST define monitored controls, evidence sources, sampling or event frequency, thresholds, drift conditions, escalation and suspension actions.
A continuous assurance service MUST identify data gaps, blind spots and periods during which assurance could not be maintained.
Observability requirements MUST be proportionate and MUST account for privacy, confidentiality, minimisation and security.
The absence of observable data MUST NOT be presented as evidence of compliant behaviour.
A trust mark MUST identify or resolve to its issuer, scope, criteria, validity, status and limitations.
A trust mark MUST NOT imply broader assurance than the underlying assessment supports.
Risk assessment MUST examine authority paths, evidence paths, dependencies, graph edges, delegated capabilities and affected parties, not only roles and processes.
A framework MUST define effect classes or thresholds that require enhanced evidence, approval, assurance, monitoring or human review.
A framework MUST consider harms arising cumulatively across repeated low-level decisions where such harms are reasonably foreseeable.
A framework SHOULD identify concentration, lock-in, exclusion, manipulation and market-power risks created by trust infrastructure.
Controls relied upon to prevent high-impact effects MUST be testable or auditable and linked to accountable owners.
A framework MUST identify affected-party classes, including persons who are not members of the trust community.
Affected parties SHOULD receive notice of consequential decisions where lawful and practicable.
A framework MUST define the explanation information available for consequential decisions, including reason codes, applicable policy and review route.
A challenge mechanism MUST permit submission of corrections, contrary evidence and claims of unauthorised action.
Review MUST be performed by an actor or process with authority to confirm, modify, reverse or remedy the decision.
A framework MUST define response and resolution time expectations proportionate to the potential harm.
A framework MUST identify which remedies are available, who may order them and how compliance is verified.
Where an effect depends on multiple governance frameworks, the frameworks SHOULD define coordination, evidence sharing, jurisdiction and fallback procedures for disputes.
A machine-actionable package MUST be traceable to the authoritative human-readable framework.
The package MUST identify its version, effective period, status and integrity mechanism.
A material package change MUST follow the framework’s revision and transition requirements.
A package MUST NOT silently broaden authority or reduce obligations relative to the authoritative framework.
Conformance tests SHOULD include valid, invalid and boundary-condition examples.
A profile claim MUST identify the specification version, profile version, applicable conformance targets, exclusions and evidence location.
A framework MUST NOT claim a profile when a mandatory category or requirement is omitted without an explicitly permitted exception.
Conformance claims MUST be scoped to an identified target and MUST NOT be generalised to related entities or transactions without evidence.
Framework conformance MUST NOT be presented as proof that every governed actor, agent, decision or transaction conforms.
An agent conformance claim MUST NOT be presented as proof that every future action of that agent is authorised or safe.
Evidence MUST be sufficient to reproduce or independently evaluate the conformance conclusion to the degree required by the claimed profile.
Exceptions MUST identify the requirement, rationale, authority, duration, risk, compensating control and review date.
A framework MUST define classification, notification, remediation, suspension and appeal procedures for non-conformance.
Governance-critical artifacts MUST be protected against unauthorised modification and rollback.
Frameworks MUST define recovery and continuity procedures for unavailable or compromised governance services.
Evidence, receipts and observability data MUST be minimised to what is necessary for governance, assurance, accountability and lawful obligations.
Access to sensitive governance records MUST be controlled, auditable and purpose-bound.
A framework MUST identify critical dependencies and define safe behaviour when they are unavailable or untrustworthy.
